does: This paper proposes ESFP-RM, a two-stage language model-based reward model that reframes reward modeling as a natural language inference task to improve RLAIF. The key contribution is identifying that masked language models with contextual explanations outperform standard autoregressive models on NLI-style reward prediction tasks. The method uses an explanation-based slot prediction framework where explanations provide context for rating predictions, demonstrating that this approach achieves superior performance on both in-distribution RLHF benchmarks and out-of-distribution scenarios compared to existing generative reward models.
connects: This work builds on the recent emergence of LM-based judging reward models, particularly generative reward models that enable efficient RLAIF by using language models to score outputs. It responds to limitations in current autoregressive reward modeling approaches by connecting to the natural language understanding literature, specifically insights from natural language inference tasks where masked language models have shown advantages. The paper draws on established NLU methods and the growing body of work on scaling properties of language models, combining these threads to improve how reward models understand and evaluate complex judgments.
enables: This research opens directions for advancing reward modeling beyond current autoregressive paradigms by demonstrating the value of explanation-based reasoning in evaluation. It points toward more robust reward models that generalize better out-of-distribution, a critical capability for real-world RLHF applications. The NLI-based perspective could inspire hybrid approaches combining masked and autoregressive objectives, and the slot-based prediction framework could extend to other evaluation domains. The work suggests that incorporating explicit reasoning and contextual explanations in reward models may be fundamental to building trustworthy AI feedback systems that scale to more complex reasoning tasks.
